messages.content:
What the student said/wrote
She yet hasn’t finished. 
What the student intended to say/write or  should have said/written
She hasn’t finished yet.1.What kind of grammar problem does the  error illustrate? (what grammar rule the  student doesn’t know yet) 
2.Is it a grammar problem related to meaning  (when to use this structure) or form (how to  use this structure in a sentence)? Why? 
3.Why did the student make this error?  (identify the reason, e.g.: L1 interference,  overgeneralization, false friends, fossilised  error, lack of practice, etc.) 
Is the following the correct answers for the above?1) It illustrates a problem with adverb placement for the adverb yet in the present perfect simple.
The present perfect simple describes an action that started in the past and has continued up to now.
We use yet in the negative to indicate that something hasn’t happened before now, but is expected to.
(Scrivener)

2) It is a problem related to form: It changes the word order by placing the adverb between the subject and the verb instead of at the end to make it a negative and indicate the activity hasn’t finished at this time. 


3)The present perfect simple doesn’t exist in Polish. This makes it harder for lower level students with L1 Polish to master them.(Sandy Millan).
Yes, those are the correct answers for the given error.

what is wrong with this sentence?:Once the task has been completed by the students the teacher can elicit justification for the answers, asking why the students utilized the form and then consolidate the lesson by going over the points of the lesson.

There are a couple of things wrong with this sentence:

1. "utilized the form" - "utilized" is a formal word and sounds awkward in this context. It would be better to use a simpler verb like "used".
2. "then consolidate the lesson" - This part of the sentence is awkwardly phrased. It would be better to rephrase it as "consolidate the lesson by reviewing the key points" or something similar.
 
A possible correction could be: "Once the task has been completed by the students, the teacher can elicit justification for their answers by asking why they used particular forms, and then consolidate the lesson by reviewing the key points."
what's wrong with this sentence?When Teresa started, her learning classes were mostly focused on grammar, lexis as well as receptive and productive skills with the main focus being grammar.

The main issue with this sentence is the redundancy of "grammar". The sentence mentions "grammar" twice, and this is not necessary since it is already implied in the phrase "learning classes". Also, it is not clear how receptive and productive skills are related to grammar in this context. Therefore, it would be better to rephrase the sentence as follows:

"When Teresa started her language learning classes, the focus was mainly on grammar and vocabulary, as well as receptive and productive skills."

what is wrong with these sentences?:To engage Teresa fully in the learning cycle and to help keep her fully engaged, activities such as journaling to engage in the reflective learning style.  To work in the activist learning style language games or an informal debate model might be used.  To utilize the theoretical learning tasks that focus on grammar or vocabulary can be utilized.

There are a few issues with these sentences: 

1. "To engage Teresa fully in the learning cycle and to help keep her fully engaged" - This is redundant. The phrase "to keep her fully engaged" is unnecessary since it is already implied in "to engage Teresa fully in the learning cycle".

2. "journaling to engage in the reflective learning style" - This phrase is not a complete sentence. It would be better to rephrase it as "Activities, such as journaling, can help engage Teresa in reflective learning."

3. "To work in the activist learning style" - This is not a complete sentence. It would be better to rephrase it as "Activities that involve working in the activist learning style, such as language games or an informal debate model, might be beneficial for Teresa."

4. "To utilize the theoretical learning tasks that focus on grammar or vocabulary can be utilized" - This sentence is redundant. It would be better to rephrase it as "Theoretical learning tasks that focus on grammar or vocabulary can be helpful for Teresa's learning."

A possible correction for all the sentences could be: 
"To fully engage Teresa in the learning cycle, activities such as journaling can help her develop reflective learning skills. In addition, activities that involve working in the activist learning style, such as language games or an informal debate model, might be beneficial. Theoretical learning tasks that focus on grammar or vocabulary can also be helpful for Teresa's learning."

what is wrong with this sentence?Utilizing activities for each of these learning styles will help Teresa be fully invested in the learning process while providing a classroom situation that brings out the strengths of her classmates, thus providing interaction and preventing the onset of boredom in learning by performing similar tasks repeatedly.

The main issue with this sentence is that it is too long and unclear. It contains several different ideas that could be better conveyed in separate sentences. Additionally, the phrase "preventing the onset of boredom in learning by performing similar tasks repeatedly" is quite wordy and could be simplified. 

A possible revision could be:
"Utilizing activities that accommodate different learning styles can help engage Teresa and her classmates in the learning process. This can lead to improved interaction and prevent boredom that may result from repetitive tasks."

what is wrong with this sentence?Since the original activity also uses ‘yet’ to form questions I would remove these and replace them with questions that focus on the negative structure to help reinforce the structure and to avoid confusion by trying to use ‘yet’ in forming present perfect questions.

The main issue with this sentence is that it is quite long and the phrasing is somewhat awkward. Additionally, it is not clear what "original activity" is being referred to, which can make the sentence confusing to read.

One possible revision could be: 
"To reinforce the structure and prevent confusion, it is suggested to replace the questions in the original activity that use 'yet' to form present perfect questions. Instead, focus on questions that utilize the negative structure." 

This revision makes the sentence clearer and more concise, and also specifies what the original activity is referring to.

what is wrong with this sentence:During the activity the students use the word ‘yet’ and the words provided in each question to formulate sentences in the  negative form using the present perfect tense.

There is nothing grammatically incorrect with this sentence. However, it could be improved in terms of style and clarity. For example, 'using' instead of 'and' would be better to link the words. 

A possible revision could be: 
"During the activity, students form sentences in the negative present perfect tense by using the word 'yet' and the words provided in each question." 

This revision makes the sentence clearer and more concise.

what is wrong with this sentence?:After covering the examples in the task the teacher should give clear instructions to work together using the words provided in each question and the word ‘yet’ to form negative sentences using the present perfect tense.

There is nothing grammatically incorrect with this sentence. However, it could be improved in terms of clarity and structure. For example, it is a bit long and could be broken up into shorter sentences to make it more readable. Additionally, it might be better to specify what task is being referred to in the sentence.

A possible revision could be:
"After reviewing the examples in the given task, the teacher should provide clear instructions for students to work together. They should use the words provided in each question, along with the word 'yet', to form negative sentences using the present perfect tense." 

This revision makes the sentence clearer, more concise, and creates a better structure which would improve the flow and readability.

Wojtek
Polish
A2
She yet hasn’t finished.
She hasn’t finished yet.

1) It illustrates a problem with adverb placement for the adverb yet in the present perfect simple.
The present perfect simple is used to show something that  started in the past and has continued up to now.
We use yet in the negative to indicate that something hasn’t happened before now, but is expected to.
(Scrivener)

2) It is a problem related to form: It changes the word order by placing the adverb between the subject and the verb instead of at the end to make it a negative and indicate the activity hasn’t finished at this time. 


3)The present perfect simple doesn’t exist in Polish. This makes it harder for lower level students with L1 Polish to master them.(Sandy Millan).
Anna
Polish
A2
K-nowledge
/ˈkɒlɪdʒ/



Knowledge
/ˈnɒlɪdʒ/ 


1) It illustrates the mispronunciation or incorrect pronunciation of the initial consonant cluster “kn” in which the “k” is silent. 
(Swan & Smith)

The student said “/k/ /ɒ/ /l/ /ɪ/ /dʒ/” pronouncing the first consonant k.
The student should have said “/n/ /ɒ/ /l/ /ɪ/ /dʒ/” since the word starts with the consonant cluster /kn/ and not the single consonant /k/.. To pronounce the /kn/ correctly the student could raise their tongue to the back of the mouth and release some air through the nose without moving the tongue. This will help in producing the silent /k/ sound and make the /n/ sound heard.

2) The error most likely is caused by L1 interference. Polish speakers have difficulty with words that have silent letters or syllables.(Swan & Smith) Polish has a more sound-to-spelling correspondence so they may try to pronounce every letter when learning. 




The activity is a controlled practice activity because it focuses on the present perfect in the negative using the word ‘yet’. The activity uses an easy pattern for the students to understand and practice the grammatical structure.
In order to reinforce the structure and prevent confusion, I would replace the questions in the original activity that use ‘yet’ to form present perfect questions. I would replace them with questions that utilize the negative structure.
During the activity students use the word ‘yet’ with the words provided in each question to formulate sentences in the  negative form using the present perfect tense.


During the activity the students use the word ‘yet’ and the words provided in each question to formulate sentences in the  negative form in the present perfect tense.


This task is to focus on accuracy. The purpose is to let the students practice making negative sentences in the present perfect tense using the word ‘yet’ accurately. The students focus on form to help with their use of the present perfect in the negative using the word ‘yet’ in developing sentences. 
After covering the examples in the task, the teacher should give clear instructions to work together. The students should use the words provided in each question, along with the word ‘yet’ to form negative sentences using the present perfect tense. While the students work together the teacher should monitor the students and provide immediate feedback in hot error correction using prompts to help students correct their mistakes. This will help internalize the correct form. Once the task has been completed by the students, the teacher can elicit justification for the answers by asking why the students used  the form, and then consolidate the lesson by going over the points of the lesson. 




Teresa has been learning English for about three years in online courses and conversation groups. She has also had some experience with English in person through conversation groups and traveling to English speaking countries.
When Teresa started her learning classes, the focus was mainly grammar and vocabulary, as well as receptive and productive skills.  Her first experience was with teachers online in private lessons to learn the basics of the grammatical structure in English. Teresa said she found this boring and it was hard at times to continue showing up for the lessons.
The majority of Teresa’s experience has been in group lessons online.  She has tried different centers who utilize different methods.  Teresa feels that the experience of interactive group lessons with activities for the entire class to work on have been the best for her. She said that the classroom discussions and interaction between the students and the teachers made the classes more enjoyable and a more productive environment for her to learn.



Teresa’s motivation to study English is intrinsic and stems from her desire to clearly communicate while traveling or having conversations with English speakers. This is different from an extrinsic motivation which might result from the need to learn English for reasons such as professional necessity.
Teresa seems to be interested in practicing both receptive and productive skills focusing more on fluency than accuracy, though she does find accuracy to be important. She expects topics in class that are relevant to real world  situations involving conversation.  While activities that improve her speaking skills and vocabulary are important, so is the clarification of new grammar and vocabulary to improve her fluency in conversation along with accuracy in the use of English. She would like time in class for speaking to work on her skills. 
Teresa has set the goal for speaking fluently in conversation using correct English and will benefit from activities that focus on communication and utilize hot error correction.



In reviewing the results of the Honey and Monford inventory Teresa showed her strongest learning preference as pragmatic. She prefers to study using practical applications and tasks such as role-playing real life situations and using targeted language to complete the tasks.
To fully engage Teresa In the learning cycle, activities such as journaling can help her develop reflective learning skills. In addition, activities that involve working in the activist learning style, such as language games or an informal debate model, might be beneficial.  Theoretical learning tasks that focus on grammar or vocabulary can also be helpful for Teresa’s learning. Utilizing activities that accommodate the different learning styles will help engage Teresa with her classmates in the learning process.  This could lead to improved interaction and the prevention of boredom that comes from performing repetitive tasks.


Scrivener, J.(2010). Teaching English Grammar: What to teach and how to teach it (pp. 166-168). Oxford, UK: Macmillan Education.

Sandy Millin. (2018, October 27). Typical Problems for Polish Learners of English.  Retrieved  June 11, 2023, from https://sandymillin.wordpress.com/2018/10/27typical-problems-for-polish-learners-of-english/.

Swan, M., & Smith, B. (2001). Learner English: A teacher’s guide to interference and other problems (2nd ed., p. 166). Cambridge, UK: Cambridge University Press.

Swan, M. & Walter, C (2014) The Good Grammar Book(pp. 60, 292). Oxford,UK: Oxford University Press.
The given text is comprehensive and there don't seem to be any issues with it.
Are there any grammar, punctuation or spelling errors
No, I didn't see any grammar, punctuation, or spelling errors in the given text.
